steps.text: You made graphs with scale of 5. Let's connect what you learned to the world around you.

params.description: Four real-world images displayed in a grid: hand showing 5 fingers, tally marks grouped by 5s, nickels (worth 5 cents each), and clock face showing 5-minute intervals.

steps.text: You've been grouping by 5s all lesson. But 5s show up everywhere in real life. Which of these uses groups of 5?
Which of these uses groups of 5? Select all that apply.
All of them! Hands have 5 fingers. Tallies group by 5s. Nickels are worth 5 cents. Clocks mark every 5 minutes. Grouping by 5s is everywhere — and now you use it for graphs too.
Scale of 5 isn't just for math class. People group by 5s all the time because it's easy to count.

elements.description: Four real-world images displayed. All four highlighted as correct selections showing groups of 5.
Four real-world images displayed: hand with 5 fingers, tally marks in groups of 5, nickels worth 5 cents, clock face with 5-minute marks. Multi-select tool active.

params.description: Simple display showing two scales side by side. Scale of 2: Each symbol = 2. Scale of 5: Each symbol = 5.

steps.text: You now know TWO scales for making graphs. If you had to show 30 items on a graph, which scale would need fewer symbols?
If you had to show 30 items on a graph, which scale would need fewer symbols?
Scale of 5. You'd need 6 symbols instead of 15. Bigger scale, fewer symbols.
You're building a toolkit. Scale of 2 works. Scale of 5 works. Soon you'll learn when to use each one.

elements.description: Display showing Scale of 2 and Scale of 5 side by side. Scale of 5 highlighted as the answer requiring fewer symbols for 30 items.
Display showing two scales: Scale of 2 (each symbol = 2) and Scale of 5 (each symbol = 5). Multiple choice tool active.

params.description: Representative graphs from the lesson displayed side by side: a picture graph with scale of 5 and a bar graph with scale of 5, both showing the same data.

steps.text: You started this unit knowing scale of 2. Now you can make graphs with scale of 5 too.
And you made BOTH picture graphs AND bar graphs. Two formats, both showing the same data.
Two scales in your toolkit. Two formats you can create. You're ready for more.
You started with scale of 1, then 2, now 5. Next time, we'll add scale of 10 to your toolkit.

elements.description: Representative graphs displayed: picture graph with scale of 5 and bar graph with scale of 5, side by side showing the same data.
